records.ety: [F. ressentir; L. pref. re- re- + sentire to feel. See Sense.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Resented; p. pr. & vb. n. Resenting.]

defs: To be sensible of; to feel
In a good sense, to take well; to receive with satisfaction.
In a bad sense, to take ill; to consider as an injury or affront; to be indignant at.
quotes: Which makes the tragical ends of noble persons more favorably resented by compassionate readers.

defs: To express or exhibit displeasure or indignation at, as by words or acts.
quotes: The good prince King James . . . bore dishonorably what he might have resented safely.

defs: To recognize; to perceive, especially as if by smelling; -- associated in meaning with sent, the older spelling of scent to smell. See Resent, v. i.
quotes: This bird of prey resented a worse than earthly savor in the soul of Saul.
Our King Henry the Seventh quickly resented his drift.

defs: To give forth an odor; to smell; to savor.
quotes: The judicious prelate will prefer a drop of the sincere milk of the word before vessels full of traditionary pottage resenting of the wild gourd of human invention.
